1:
The chapter opens on a study in contrasts, and the witnesses fasten on the single word that overturns all the honours heaped before it: but he was a leper. Matthew Henry sets Naaman beside the woman of Canaan whose crumb fell from Israel's table (Matt. 15): one miracle Elisha works for a Syrian, for God does good to all and will have all men to be saved (1 Tim. 2:4). Naaman was great, honourable, a favourite of his prince and a mighty man of valour, yet, as Henry quotes bishop Hall, "the basest slave in Syria would not change skins with him." No greatness, honour, valour, or victory can set a man beyond the reach of the sorest calamities of human life; every man has "some but or other in his character," some allay to his grandeur, some damp to his joy.

The grammatical-historical commentators frame the history and the theology. Charles Ellicott hears in the very name Naaman ("beauty") a title of the sun-god, and marks the high prophetic view of the narrator: it is Jehovah, not Hadad or Rimmon, who "had given deliverance unto Syria" and gives victory to Syria as well as to Israel (Amos 9:7). Such, Henry adds, is the correct language of Scripture, while common historians "plainly show that God is not in all their thoughts." With the Midrash, both Ellicott and Adam Clarke identify Naaman with the man who "drew the bow at a venture" and slew Ahab (1 Kings 22:34), so that the deliverance was Syria's victory over Israel; and, as Thenius observes, the victor over Israel is here made to seek, and to find, his only help in Israel.

John Gill attends to the setting, taking the reigning Syrian to be Benhadad, though some make Hazael his successor the king in view. Beneath their differences the witnesses converge on Clarke's pastoral note: God often, in the course of his providence, permits great defects to be associated with great eminence, "that he may hide pride from man, and cause him to think soberly of himself." The leprosy that disgraces the great captain is the very hook by which grace will draw him; the loathsome disease that makes him a burden to himself will prove the occasion of his soul's healing.
2:
Providence turns a raid into a rescue. The witnesses dwell on the strange mercy hidden in the captive girl's misfortune. Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew precisely: the Syrians "had gone out in marauding bands" (gedudim) and carried off from the land of Israel a little maid, who came to wait upon Naaman's wife. Charles Ellicott notes that these companies were not a regular army but an incursion of freebooters, and points to the Phoenician traffic in Jewish slaves (Joel 3:6) as the dark commerce of the age. John Gill agrees that these were not regular troops but "a sort of banditti of robbers" making excursions to plunder what booty they could.

It is Adam Clarke who draws out the deepest note, meditating on "the goodness and the severity of the Divine providence." Affectionate and pious parents are stripped of their promising daughter by a set of lawless ruffians, "without the smallest prospect that she should have any lot in life but that of misery, infamy, and woe." Yet the God who permitted the parents to be robbed did not permit the child to be forsaken: her decent, orderly behaviour, "the consequence of her sober and pious education," won her a place of trust where her servitude was easy and her person safe. "If God permitted the parents to be deprived of their pious child by the hands of ruffians, he did not permit the child to be without a guardian."

The one small figure thus becomes the hinge of the whole history. Torn from home, she is set down in the house of Syria's greatest soldier at the one moment and place where her knowledge of Israel's prophet could work a nation's blessing. The witnesses see in her the pattern of the scattered people of God whose very dispersion has often proved, in Henry's phrase from the previous verse, "the happy occasion of the diffusion of the knowledge of God." What the marauders meant for harm, and what her parents wept over as loss, the Lord had woven into a design of grace that would reach from a slave-girl's whisper to a captain's conversion.
3:
The captive's wish is the gospel in miniature, and the witnesses linger over both its faith and its very words. Charles Ellicott examines the opening cry, 'ahalê, "O that! Would God!" reckoning it a cousin to 'ashrê, "O the bliss of!" so that the maid breathes something like "O the delight, would that my lord were before the prophet in Samaria!" Keil and Delitzsch weigh the crucial verb: to "recover from leprosy" ('asaph) means literally to receive back, and they explain it from Miriam's case (Num. 12:14), where the same word marks her reception again into the camp from which leprosy had shut her out. Since in Israel lepers were excluded from society, restoration to society implied restoration to health, and so the word for "receiving back" came to mean "healing."

Here the grammatical witnesses record a quiet disagreement. Ellicott notes that Thenius, missing the phrase "from leprosy" in the Miriam passage, argues that the real sense is "to take a person away from leprosy," as one delivered up to it. K&D prefer the reception-into-fellowship idea; either way the girl's confidence is unshaken: the man of God "would free him from his leprosy." John Gill imagines the scene, the mistress perhaps lamenting her husband's case as desperate and incurable, and the servant speaking her hope into that despair.

Adam Clarke lifts the eyes from grammar to grace: "Here the mystery of the Divine providence begins to develop itself. By the captivity of this little maid, one Syrian family at least, and that one of the most considerable in the Syrian empire, is brought to the knowledge of the true God." The witnesses agree in the wonder of it. A homesick girl, with no power but the knowledge of what Elisha had done, becomes the herald of Israel's God to the court of Damascus. Her few words carry more than the physicians of Syria could compass, for she alone in that house knows where healing is to be had, and she has the heart to tell it even to the master who holds her captive. In her few words, the witnesses agree, the whole after-history is already contained.
4:
A single sentence of report carries the story from the servants' quarters to the throne, and the witnesses debate only who spoke it. The Hebrew reads simply that "one went in and told his lord." John Gill lays out the choices: either a servant overheard the maid's words to her mistress and carried them in, "or rather, Naaman went and told his lord the king of Syria; for as this was said to his wife, no doubt she told it to her husband," which, Gill adds, "is the sense of most Jewish commentators." Charles Ellicott reads the clause the same way, "and he (that is, Naaman) went in" into the palace, while noting that some manuscripts have "and she went in and told," making the mistress the messenger.

The phrase "thus and thus said the maid" the commentators take as the narrator's shorthand. Ellicott explains that it is used "to avoid repetition of her actual words," already given in the verse before. Adam Clarke seizes on what that shorthand implies about the girl's credit: "So well had this little pious maid conducted herself, that her words are credited; and credited so fully, that an embassy from the king of Syria to the king of Israel is founded upon them!" Gill presses the same grammar the other way, observing that because the report was spoken first to the mistress and only then borne to the throne, "the following words require" that it was Naaman himself who carried it in, "and is the sense of most Jewish commentators" — the healed man's own lips setting the machinery of state in motion.

That is the marvel the witnesses will not let pass. The testimony of a captive child, without rank or proof, is weighed in the balances of a royal court and found worth an international embassy. Naaman does not despise the counsel for the meanness of its source, as Henry observed of him; when it touches his healing he will act on a slave-girl's word. The great machinery of kings and letters and treasure that follows is set in motion by a faithful servant who simply told what she knew of the glory of God and the honour of his prophet — a reminder that the lowliest witness, credibly borne, may move the highest powers.
5:
The king of Syria acts at once, and the witnesses turn to the weight of the purse he sends. John Wesley supplies the king's reasoning in a line: "It was very natural for a king to suppose, that the king of Israel could do more than any of his subjects" — and so the embassy is misdirected from the first, aimed at a throne rather than at a prophet. Keil and Delitzsch tally the gift as "quite in keeping with Naaman's position": ten talents of silver, six thousand shekels of gold (two talents), and ten changes of costly raiment, "a present still highly valued in the East," no more than the object warranted, namely deliverance from a slowly fatal malady.

Over the arithmetic the grammatical witnesses divide. Adam Clarke reckons the silver and gold together at more than fourteen thousand pounds sterling, "besides the value of the ten caftans," a princely present that "shows us at once how high Naaman stood in the esteem of his master." Charles Ellicott works the same sums but recoils from the total: "The total sum appears much too large, and the numbers are probably corrupt, as is so often the case." He adds a historian's touch, that the letter was likely written "in that old Aramean script" close kin to the old Phoenician and Hebrew characters of the Moabite stone, and that the "changes of raiment" were holiday suits, habits de fête, and that the letter went "with him" meaning, literally, in his hand — the idiom he compares to "filling the hand for Jehovah" with an offering (1 Chron. 29:5). John Gill notes only that the king "urged him by all means to go directly to Samaria."

Beneath the debate over talents and shekels the witnesses agree on the spiritual irony. Naaman goes up laden with the wealth of Damascus to buy what gold cannot purchase; he thinks a cure may be had for a price, and carries treasure enough to make a prophet rich. The very magnificence of the present betrays the error the story will correct: that healing is a gift of the God of Israel, to be received, as Henry says, only "as beggars for a free gift, not as lords to demand or purchase."
6:
The letter names only its errand — "that thou mayest recover him of his leprosy" — and the witnesses read in that terse demand the seeds of the coming alarm. Charles Ellicott observes that only the principal sentence of the letter is given, and that its very tone "pre-supposes a not altogether hostile relation between the two kings." John Gill likewise notes that the contents are reported "only so far as it concerned Naaman and his case." The demand that one king should "recover" a leper, laid as a plain expectation upon another, is the spark that will make Jehoram rend his clothes.

The chief labour of the witnesses here is to fix the moment in Israel's history, and on this the grammatical school openly disagrees. Ellicott argues that the friendly footing between the courts, and the next verse's fear of a "quarrel," point to "the time of comparative lull" after the disastrous expedition to Ramoth-gilead (1 Kings 22) and the short reign of Ahaziah — that is, to the reign of Jehoram, not to that of Jehoahaz, under whom Israel was wholly crushed by Syria (2 Kings 13:3-7). He records the rival view of Schenkel, who takes the Syrian inroads of the chapter to indicate the reign of Jehu, making Hazael the writer of the letter, since Hazael was personally acquainted with Elisha. But, Ellicott answers with Thenius, that theory "forgets that the relations between Jehu and Syria were throughout strained to the last degree," so that so friendly a passage between the kings "is not to be thought of." The very phrase that opens the letter, Ellicott adds — "And now," continuing an omitted passage — betrays that only its principal sentence is preserved, the courtesies of address having dropped away, so that the demand stands bare and abrupt, the more easily to be misheard as a threat.

The dispute may seem a scholar's quarrel, yet it guards a real point the witnesses care to make: this is history, anchored in a datable lull between wars, not a floating legend. The letter that assumes cordiality could only have passed in a season of uneasy peace; and into that fragile calm the God of Israel is about to work a cure that will preach his sole deity to Israel's enemy.
7:
The king of Israel reads, and tears his robe. The witnesses agree that his panic springs from a truth he half-remembers: that only God can heal the leper. Keil and Delitzsch render his cry, "Am I God, to be able to kill and make alive?" — am I omnipotent like God (Deut. 32:39; 1 Sam. 2:6)? Adam Clarke makes the same point the pivot of the scene: the king spoke "under the conviction that God alone could cure the leprosy, which indeed was universally acknowledged," a maxim as firm among the Syrians as among the Israelites, "for the disorder was equally prevalent in both countries, and in both equally incurable."

Charles Ellicott notes that Jehoram rends his clothes "as if he had heard blasphemy" (Matt. 26:65), and that leprosy was reckoned "a kind of living death," the leper being as one dead whose flesh is half consumed (Num. 12:12). The plural verbs of "consider and see" he explains as the king turning to his grandees, in whose presence the letter would be read. John Gill adds that some take the tearing of the clothes for distress at the blasphemy of requiring what God alone can do, others for sheer perplexity of mind.

From this shared conviction the witnesses trace the king's fatal misreading. Clarke sets out the logic: because none but God could cure the leprosy, Jehoram infers "that the Syrian king sought a quarrel with him, in desiring him to do a work which God only could do, and then declaring war upon him because he did not do it." Ellicott adds the political undertone, that Jehoram "was hardly in a position to renew the war after the severe defeat of his father." Yet the deepest note is the one Henry sounded: the king's real error is that he had made himself a stranger to the prophet. "The captive maid had him more in her thoughts than the king had." Confronted with an impossible demand, the throne despairs where a slave-girl had believed, and the man who could have named Elisha in a moment is undone by forgetting that there was a prophet in Israel.
8:
Where the king sees only threat, the prophet sees opportunity. Hearing that Jehoram had rent his clothes, Elisha sends word: "Let him come now to me, and he shall know that there is a prophet in Israel." Adam Clarke catches the reassuring tone of the message — "Do not be afflicted; the matter belongs to me, as the prophet of the Most High; send him to me, and he shall know that I am such." Charles Ellicott lays the stress where the Hebrew does, on the words there is a prophet, "as his message pre-supposes": Israel has what Syria's gods and Israel's own frightened king cannot supply.

The witnesses press the question the scene forces: why did Jehoram not think of Elisha at once? Ellicott offers two reasons and a caution. First, "king and prophet were not on good terms with each other" (2 Kings 3:14). Second, "Elisha had not as yet done any miracle of this sort," so that no precedent stood ready to the king's mind. And third, more gently, "his apprehensions may have made the king unable, for the moment, to think at all." John Gill notes simply that the prophet acted "upon what account" the king had torn his robe — moved by the report of that public despair. Elisha, Ellicott notes, was himself in Samaria at the time and "would hear of the coming of the great Syrian captain and of the king's alarm," so that the prophet, unbidden, reaches out to steady the panic he had not caused.

Henry had already drawn the marrow of it in the preceding verses: Elisha was "willing to do any thing to make his prince easy, though he was neglected and his former good services were forgotten by him." The prophet covets the errand not for his own honour but for God's, that all — Syrian and Israelite, king and court — might know "that there was a prophet in Israel, though obscure and overlooked." So the witnesses agree in the shape of the moment: the throne is paralysed by a demand it cannot meet, and the neglected man of God quietly offers to meet it, that the living God of Israel might be known where he had been forgotten.
9:
Naaman arrives "with his horses and with his chariot," and the witnesses read the whole drama of pride in that pomp at a poor prophet's door. Adam Clarke catches the incongruity in a phrase: "a leper sitting in state, and affecting it!" Matthew Henry turns it to a lesson: those who showed the prophets little respect at other times "were very complaisant to them when they needed them," and Naaman now "attended at Elisha's door as a beggar for an alms." So must all who would be cleansed of the spiritual leprosy "wait at Wisdom's gate, and watch at the posts of her doors" (Prov. 8:34). Yet the prophet gave his answer "without any formality, would not go to the door to him, lest he should seem too much pleased with the honour done him, but sent a messenger."

Why the prophet stayed within and sent word by a servant becomes the chapter's first real debate, and Keil and Delitzsch canvass the field. The reason "is not to be sought for in the legal prohibition of intercourse with lepers, as Ephraem Syrus supposes, nor in his fear of the leper, as Thenius thinks, nor even in the wish to magnify the miracle," as Cornelius a Lapide imagines, "but simply in Naaman's state of mind" — his pride, betrayed by the outburst that follows. Henry agrees that Elisha "knew Naaman to be a proud man, and would let him know that before the great God all men stand upon the same level." Charles Ellicott dissents on the reading of Naaman's heart: "as Thenius says, there is no trace of pride about Naaman," and the prophet's aloofness is better explained by the reverence that held petitioners back from a great seer's presence, or by his simply having a messenger at command. John Gill allows several motives together — a wish to be retired, perhaps at prayer, and a holy contempt for worldly grandeur.

Beneath the dispute the witnesses converge on the design of it. The great captain who came expecting to be met with ceremony is met instead by a plain command through a servant, that the cure might carry no odour of flattery and no debt to human greatness. Whether the prophet meant chiefly to humble a proud man or only to keep the miracle wholly God's, the effect is one: Naaman must learn at the threshold that healing is not bought with pomp, but received in humility from the God of Israel.
10:
The cure comes as a command, not a ceremony: "Go and wash in Jordan seven times." The witnesses agree that the water is a sign, not a medicine. Charles Ellicott puts it flatly with Bähr: "The Syrians knew as well as the Israelites that the Jordan could not heal leprosy." The bare command "would make it clear that Naaman was not cured by any external means applied by the prophet"; he was to understand that "he was healed by the God of Israel, at His prophet's prayer." Adam Clarke agrees that "the waters of Jordan had no tendency to remove this disorder, but God chose to make them the means by which he would convey his healing power," for the author of life and health "has a right to dispense his gifts by whatsoever means he pleases."

Keil and Delitzsch weigh the words: "thy flesh shall come again to thee," the verb ishab meaning return, since the leprosy had changed sound flesh into festering matter. John Wesley reads the prophet's method as a double discipline, sending the command "partly to exercise Naaman's faith and obedience; partly for the honour of his religion, that it might appear he sought not his own glory and profit, but only God's honour, and the good of men." John Gill notes that Elisha "did not go out to him, choosing to be retired," perhaps at prayer for the cure, and so to mortify the pride of Naaman. Ellicott observes that the opening "Go" is an infinitive standing for the imperative, a curt marching-order rather than a courtesy, and that its very plainness "would make it clear that Naaman was not cured by any external means applied by the prophet" — the command itself is stripped of everything that might rob God of the glory.

The sevenfold washing the witnesses will unfold more fully at the healing itself, but here they already mark its meaning: seven is the signature of the works of God, stamping the cure as his and not the river's. The whole prescription is contrived to strip the miracle of every rival cause — no touch, no incantation, no virtue in the stream — so that when the flesh returns clean, Naaman can thank neither Jordan nor the prophet's hand, but only the God of Israel who commanded and healed.
11:
Naaman's wrath is the portrait of wounded pride, and Adam Clarke anatomizes it word by word. "God's ways are not as our ways," he begins; Naaman "expected to be treated with great ceremony, and instead of humbling himself before the Lord's prophet, he expected the prophet of the Lord to humble himself before him!" Clarke unfolds the four clauses of the great man's imagined cure: the prophet "will surely come out to me" and never send a servant; "and stand" present himself before me as a servant to hear his God's orders; "and call on the name of Jehovah his God," so that both God and prophet do me honour; "and strike his hand over the place," for surely no healing virtue could pass without contact. "Had he done these things, then the leper might have been recovered."

Charles Ellicott corrects the gesture and softens nothing of the pride: the prophet was expected not to strike but to "wave his hand toward the place," for Naaman assumed even the healer would not touch the unclean spot. John Wesley reduces the whole tempest to its root — Naaman was wroth, "supposing himself despised by the prophet." Ellicott hears the same wounded fancy in the opening words, rendering "I thought" as "I said to myself," the great man rehearsing in his own mind a ceremony that never came. John Gill notes only that he was angry "on more accounts than one."

Henry had already named the folly the witnesses here expose: Naaman had built in his mind a whole scheme of how the cure should be wrought, "and, because the thing was not done just thus, he fell into a passion," forgetting that he was a leper who ought not to insist on the punctilios of honour, and a petitioner suing for a favour he could not demand. "A cure will not content him unless he be cured with ceremony." So the witnesses agree that Naaman's anger is not really at Jordan or at the servant, but at a God who will heal only the humble; and the man stands, for a moment, on the edge of forfeiting his own mercy for the sake of his dignity.
12:
Naaman's second grievance is patriotic: are not Damascus's rivers better than all the waters of Israel? The witnesses spend much labour identifying the streams and more in exposing the pride beneath the question. Charles Ellicott names them: Abana (the Hebrew margin and many manuscripts read Amana) is the present Barada, "the cold," descending from the Anti-Lebanon through Damascus in seven streams; Pharpar is the Nahr el-Awaj, "the swift," coming down from great Hermon. Both are clear mountain waters, "whereas the Jordan is turbid and discoloured," and "Damascus is still famous for its wholesome water." Adam Clarke and John Gill canvass the older guesses — the Chrysorrhoas, "the golden stream," the Orontes — and cite Maundrell's travels, who could find no trace of the ancient names but described the Barrady watering the gardens of Damascus.

The commentators note how the geography exposes the heart. Ellicott hears the real complaint: "If mere washing in a river be enough, it were easy to do that at home, and to much better advantage." John Wesley answers the complaint on the spot: "Is there not as great a virtue in them to this purpose? But he should have considered that the cure was not to be wrought by the water, but by the power of God." That is the pivot on which all the witnesses turn.

Henry drives it home. Naaman "might wash in them and be clean from dirt, but not wash in them and be clean from leprosy," for Jordan "belonged to Israel's God, from whom he was to expect the cure, and not from the gods of Damascus." In a miraculous cure "relation to God was much more considerable than the depth of the channel or the beauty of the stream." It is "common for those that are wise in their own conceit to look with contempt on the dictates of divine wisdom, and to prefer their own fancies before them." So Naaman "turned away in a rage," and Henry's warning stands over him: "Those that observe lying vanities forsake their own mercies" — proud men "are the worst enemies to themselves, and forego their own redemption."
13:
The turning point comes not from the prophet but from Naaman's own servants, and the witnesses honour both their courage and their argument. Their appeal is tender: "My father, if the prophet had bid thee do some great thing, wouldest thou not have done it? how much rather then, when he saith to thee, Wash, and be clean?" Adam Clarke lays out the reasoning: had the prophet appointed "something very difficult in itself, and very expensive to thee, wouldst thou not have done it? With much greater reason shouldst thou do what will occupy little time, be no expense, and is easy to be performed." Charles Ellicott weighs the address "my father" as "a title implying at once respect and affection" (2 Kings 6:21), though he notes some would read the Hebrew as "if." John Wesley prefers "our father," the plural showing "their reverence and affection to him."

Henry, in the fuller reflection of the preceding verses, makes these servants the moral of the scene. Their reproof "was very modest and respectful" and "very rational and considerate"; had they instead flattered their master's rage "and offered to avenge his quarrel upon the prophet, how mischievous would the consequences have been!" Instead they took the prophet's part. "It is a great mercy to have those about us that will be free with us, and faithfully tell us of our faults and follies, though they be our inferiors." As "we should be deaf to the counsel of the ungodly, though given by the greatest names, so we should have our ear open to good advice, though brought us by those below us: no matter who speaks, if the thing be well said."

The witnesses see the gospel pressed home in the servants' logic. "When diseased sinners come to this, that they are content to do any thing for a cure, then, and not till then, there begin to be some hopes of them." The remedy is so plain that refusal is inexcusable: "It is but, Believe, and be saved — Repent, and be pardoned — Wash, and be clean." Naaman, so lately a leper insisting on his dignity, is recalled to his own healing by the humblest voices in his train.
14:
Naaman obeys, and is clean. The witnesses read the sevenfold dipping as the seal of God upon the work. Keil and Delitzsch explain the number: "seven times, to show that the healing was a work of God, for seven is the stamp of the works of God," and his flesh "became sound like the flesh of a little boy." Charles Ellicott agrees that seven "was significant of the Divine covenant with Israel," and adds a striking parallel from the Assyrian tablets, an exorcism from the age of Sargon of Agade in which the god Merodach is told to send a sick man to bathe in the sacred waters at the mouth of the Euphrates — so that Elisha "acted in accordance with ancient Semitic belief as to the healing virtue of running streams," though here the virtue is God's alone. Adam Clarke lingers on the wonder of the change: "the loathsome scurf was now entirely removed; his flesh assumed the appearance and health of youth. How mighty is God! What great things can he do by the simplest and feeblest of means!"

Henry, in the fuller comment, notes the imperfect faith with which the cure was received: whereas the prophet bade him wash seven times, Naaman "did but dip himself so many times, as lightly as he could," with "no great faith and resolution." "However, God was pleased so far to honour himself and his word as to make that effectual," so that "his flesh came again, like the flesh of a child, to his great surprise and joy." John Gill marks simply that he did "according to the saying of the man of God."

Here the witnesses gather their teaching. This is what men get "by yielding to the will of God, by attending to his institutions." The way of cleansing "through the blood and by the Spirit of Christ, through faith alone in his name," does not flatter self as Naaman wished to be flattered; "human wisdom thinks it can supply wiser and better methods." But the believer "applies for salvation, not neglecting, altering, or adding to the Saviour's directions," and so is made clean, while others, neglecting them, "live and die in the leprosy of sin."
15:
The cleansed captain returns a convert, and the witnesses rejoice in his confession while weighing how far his knowledge yet reaches. Matthew Henry hears in "there is no God in all the earth but in Israel" a "noble confession," and remembers that of the ten lepers Christ cleansed, only the Samaritan returned to give thanks (Luke 17:16). "Had he seen other lepers cleansed, perhaps the sight would not have convinced him; but the mercy of the cure affected him more than the miracle of it. Those are best able to speak of the power of divine grace who have themselves experienced it."

The grammatical-historical witnesses add a note of realism to the praise. Charles Ellicott observes that Naaman, "like most of his contemporaries, Jewish as well as Syrian, believed in locally restricted deities"; having felt the powerlessness of the gods of Syria and the potency of Jehovah, "he concludes that there is no god anywhere save in the land of Israel." "What a mark of historic truth appears in this representation!" The local conception still clings to him — a thing the next verses will show. Keil and Delitzsch note that he came with all his train, acknowledged the God of Israel, and pressed upon the prophet a "blessing," a present (Gen. 33:11; 1 Sam. 25:27), which Elisha steadfastly refused, "that he might avoid all appearance of selfishness, by which the false prophets were actuated."

Adam Clarke marks the transformation in Naaman's very bearing: he who came "more proud and dictatorial" now "stood before him" truly humbled, having "left all his state behind him," for "the most excellent of the earth are the most humble." John Gill notes that Naaman came back "to give thanks to God for the cure," sensible "it was from the Lord"; and Gill marks the reversal, that the man who came to buy a cure now stands to give thanks, and by the prophet to thank the God who wrought it. So the witnesses hold two truths together: the confession is genuine and glad, the fruit of experienced grace, yet it is a young convert's confession still shadowed by the old geography of the gods — a soul truly turned, but not yet fully taught.
16:
Elisha's refusal is emphatic and oath-bound: "As the LORD liveth, before whom I stand, I will receive none." The witnesses agree that the motive is the honour of God, not any scruple about gifts as such. John Wesley states the distinction cleanly: Elisha refused "not that he thought it unlawful to receive presents, which he did receive from others, but because of the special circumstances of the case; this being much for the honour of God, that the Syrians should see the generous piety of his ministers, and how much they despised all that worldly wealth which the prophets of the Gentiles so greedily sought after." Adam Clarke reasons the same way: had he taken the gift, "it might have appeared to the Syrians that he had taken it as a remuneration for the cure; but as God alone did the work, he alone should have all the glory."

Charles Ellicott reaches for the New Testament parallel, noting that Theodoret compares our Lord's charge, "Freely ye have received, freely give" (Matt. 10:8; compare Acts 8:20). Such, he thinks, may have been Elisha's feeling; and his refusal, "strongly contrasting with the conduct of ordinary prophets, Israelite and heathen" (1 Sam. 9), "would make a deep impression upon Naaman and his retinue." Keil and Delitzsch note the prophet's firmness under "all his urging," and John Gill observes that Elisha swears "by the Lord, whose minister and prophet he was."

Henry gathers the threads: the prophet would not have this new convert say, "I have made Elisha rich" (as Abram would not enrich himself from the king of Sodom, Gen. 14:23), that it might be "for the honour of God to show this new convert that the servants of the God of Israel were taught to look upon the wealth of this world with a holy contempt." So the witnesses stand as one: the disinterested refusal is itself a sermon, confirming Naaman in his new faith that there is no God but in Israel, and setting the servant of Jehovah in sharp relief against every mercenary priest of the nations.
17:
Naaman's next request opens the chapter's largest exegetical debate: what did he mean by "two mules' burden of earth"? The grammatical-historical reading takes it literally. Charles Ellicott finds it "natural for Naaman, with his local idea of divinity, to make this request," wishing to worship the God of Israel "on the soil of Israel, Jehovah's own land," and so to build his altar upon a foundation of this earth, or of it (Ex. 20:24). Keil and Delitzsch read it the same way, and John Gill reports the older opinion that Naaman, in "a sort of superstitious way," conceived a holiness in the very ground the prophet trod, so that an altar of it "would show that he was in the faith of the same God."

Against this stands the reading of Adam Clarke and John Wesley, who take "earth" for the silver and gold Naaman had brought. Clarke lays out both interpretations and inclines to the second, pressing the difficulty of the first: why beg leave for earth he might have taken up anywhere on the border, and "what sort of an altar could he build with two mules' burden of earth, carried from Samaria to Damascus?" On the other reading, Naaman calls his treasure "earth" or "dust" in scorn of it — as the prophets call silver and gold "thick clay" (Hab. 2:6), as Tyre "heaped up silver as the dust" (Zech. 9:3), as Solomon made silver "as stones" at Jerusalem (2 Chron. 1:15) — and asks that it be given to the prophet's servant, since Elisha would take nothing. Wesley agrees: "he seems to farm the money which he brought with him, to express how little value he now set upon it," for "what altar could be built of the earth which two mules could carry?"

Whichever way the request is read, the witnesses find one heart in it. Naaman binds his gratitude to a vow: "thy servant will henceforth offer neither burnt offering nor sacrifice unto other gods, but unto the LORD." Henry sees him "over-doing it" in his zeal for the very soil of Israel, forgetting "that all the earth is the Lord's and the fulness thereof," yet reads the excess kindly, as the transport of a fresh affection. The cleansing of his leprosy has cleansed him of his idolatry, a more dangerous disease; and the man who lately scorned the waters of Israel now cannot bear to worship Israel's God without a token of Israel's ground.
18:
Naaman's one reservation — that he must still attend his master into the house of Rimmon — draws the witnesses into their sharpest moral debate. First they identify the god. Charles Ellicott explains Rimmon as the Assyrian Rammanu, from a root meaning "to thunder," the storm-god of the atmosphere, armed with the thunderbolt, whom the Assyrians identified with the Aramean Hadad (compare Hadad-rimmon, Zech. 12:11, and Tabrimon, 1 Kings 15:18). The king's "leaning on my hand" he takes for the attendance of a favourite grandee (2 Kings 7:2).

On the substance the commentators divide. Adam Clarke, following Dr. John Lightfoot, "the most able Hebraist of his time," insists the verbs should be read in the past tense: "for that when my master hath gone into the house of Rimmon, and I also have bowed myself, the Lord pardon thy servant" — so that Naaman asks forgiveness for his past idolatry, not license for future compromise. "To admit the common interpretation is to admit the doctrine of indulgences, and that we may do evil that good may come." Keil and Delitzsch, with most, read it of the future: Naaman, not asking permission but only that "Jehovah might forgive him" in this one unavoidable appearance, receives from Elisha neither approval nor condemnation, only "Go in peace." The prophet could do no more "without a special command from God" than commend the young believer to the guidance of the Lord's grace. Ellicott agrees the request "is not, of course, to be judged by a Christian standard," and cites Reuss: "Naaman durst not profess conversion before the king; so he asks leave to go on assisting at the national rites." John Wesley and John Gill note the mechanics — the king leaning on Naaman's arm "for state, or for support" — Gill even wondering whether the words are Naaman's own prayer to the true Jehovah.

The witnesses converge on the pastoral principle even where they differ on the grammar. Henry, judging the reservation "not justifiable" though perhaps excusable, presses the searching application: "If, in covenanting with God, we make a reservation for any known sin, that reservation is a defeasance of his covenant. We must cast away all our transgressions, and not except any house of Rimmon." Whether Naaman confessed a past bondage or begged indulgence for a future one, the story leaves the reader with the harder question turned upon himself.
19:
Elisha dismisses the convert with two words, "Go in peace," and the witnesses read much into their brevity. Charles Ellicott attends first to the phrase that follows, that Naaman "departed a little way" — a kibrah of ground (Gen. 35:16), which "seems to mean a length of ground, a certain distance, without defining exactly how far." Had it been a parasang, as the Syriac renders, "Gehazi could not have overtaken the company so easily" — a small note that quietly sets the stage for the sequel.

The verse's real interest lies in a textual variant that touches the whole question of the previous verse. Adam Clarke records "a most singular and important reading" in one of De Rossi's manuscripts, which notes in the margin that one should read lo, "not," instead of lo, "to him." On that reading the prophet's answer becomes a firm refusal: Naaman does ask leave to worship in Rimmon's temple, "to which the prophet answers, No; go in peace" — that is, "maintain thy holy resolutions, be a consistent worshipper of the true God, and avoid all idolatrous practices." Clarke notes a second manuscript that appears to have wavered over the same word, having first written "to him" and then seemed to correct it. Clarke sees in the variant a hint that Naaman "did ask permission from the prophet to worship in Rimmon's temple," and that the corrected reading would have the prophet plainly deny it. John Gill passes the whole controversy by, taking the words at their simplest: the prophet said to Naaman, "Go in peace," a benediction rather than a verdict.

So the witnesses leave the ending deliberately open. Read one way, Elisha tenderly forbears to bind a young convert's conscience beyond what God had revealed; read the other, he gently but clearly forbids the compromise. Either way the pastoral wisdom Henry drew from the scene stands: "Young converts must be tenderly dealt with." The prophet neither flatters Naaman's weakness nor crushes his new faith, but commits him, with a blessing of peace, to the further teaching of the God who had healed him.
20:
The scene turns dark as the prophet's own servant covets what Naaman offered and Elisha refused. Matthew Henry sets the contrast in bold relief: Naaman, "a courtier, a soldier, had many servants, and we read how wise and good they were; Elisha, a holy prophet, has but one servant, and he proves a base, lying, naughty fellow." "Even Christ himself had a Judas among his followers. The means of grace cannot give grace. The nearer the church the further from God." At the root of Gehazi's sin Henry finds "the love of money, that root of all evil" (1 Tim. 6:10); as bishop Hall said, "his heart was packed up in Naaman's chests, and he must run after him to fetch it." "Multitudes, by coveting worldly wealth, have erred from the faith and pierced themselves with many sorrows."

The grammatical witnesses fix the words and the motive. Keil and Delitzsch render his oath, "as truly as Jehovah liveth, assuredly I run after him," and note that the desire rose in him for a portion of the presents his master had refused. Charles Ellicott hears Gehazi justifying himself "on the principle of spoiling the Egyptians," and reads the true force of the oath, "by the life of Jehovah, but I will run." His scorn drips in the phrase "this Syrian." John Wesley underlines the profanity of the vow: Gehazi "swears, that he might have some pretence for the action; not considering that to swear to do any wicked action is so far from excusing it, that it makes it much worse." John Gill notes the calculation was made "within himself," a scheme hatched in secret.

Henry catalogues the many strands twisted into the one sin: covetousness, and the pride of thinking himself wiser than his master for refusing gold; a grudging envy of Elisha's generosity to a stranger "though it was for the good of his soul"; and worst, the danger of "alienating Naaman from that holy religion which he had espoused," leaving the new convert to think, like Paul's slanderers, that the prophet "being crafty caught him with guile." The witnesses agree that Gehazi's crime is not merely theft but sacrilege — greed dressed in the prophet's name, threatening to undo before the Syrian the very lesson Elisha's refusal had just taught.
21:
The chase overtakes the convert, and the witnesses mark the courtesy of the healed captain against the fraud of the running servant. Keil and Delitzsch describe the meeting: "no sooner did Naaman see Gehazi running after him than he sprang quickly down from his chariot in reverential gratitude to the prophet." Adam Clarke presses the humility of the great man: "He treats even the prophet's servant with the profoundest respect, alights from his chariot, and goes to meet him." Charles Ellicott adds that the phrase, literally "fell from off the chariot," is "an Oriental mark of respect" denoting haste (Gen. 24:64).

Naaman's first word is anxiety for the prophet's welfare. His question, ha-shalom, "Is all well?" Clarke renders "Is it peace, or prosperity?" — the common courteous greeting of the East. Ellicott catches the solicitude beneath it: "Naaman feared something might have befallen the prophet." John Gill notes only that Gehazi "followed after Naaman" as fast as he could. Keil and Delitzsch add that Naaman, out of reverence to the man of God, could not sit still while the prophet's servant ran toward him, but leapt down at once, the same eager stooping of the great to the lowly that has run through the whole story — the captain who once expected the prophet to come out and bow before him now bows himself before a running messenger.

The contrast is the whole point the witnesses draw. Here is the once-proud Syrian, freshly cleansed, leaping from his chariot in reverence to greet a servant, his heart so bound to Elisha that his first thought is fear for the prophet's safety. And here is the prophet's own attendant, come not in gratitude but in greed, about to trade on that very reverence with a lie. The Gentile convert honours the man of God he has known but a day; the Israelite who has stood daily before him is running to dishonour him. The scene sets the grace-warmed heart of the outsider beside the covetous heart of the insider, and the reader feels the reversal before a single false word is spoken.
22:
Gehazi's answer is a swift, complete fabrication, and the witnesses note both its smoothness and the reality it exploits. "All is well," he begins — the very word of peace Naaman had spoken now turned into the opening of a lie. He invents an errand for his master: two young men of the sons of the prophets are just come from mount Ephraim, and for them he begs "a talent of silver, and two changes of garments." Adam Clarke observes that the tale leaned on something plausible, for "there was probably a school of the prophets at this mount." Charles Ellicott identifies the place as "the hill-country of Ephraim," the highlands where Gilgal and Bethel were situate, and notes that the "changes of garments" are the same festal suits named in the embassy's original present.

John Gill marks the servile smoothness of the opening, Gehazi assuring Naaman he "need give himself no uneasiness at the coming and sight of him." He echoes back the very greeting Naaman had spoken, "All is well," the word shalom that Clarke calls "a common mode of address and answer" — peace on his lips while theft is in his heart. The lie is well made: it names a real institution, asks a modest sum, and clothes fresh covetousness in the dress of charity to poor scholars. As Henry noted in the fuller comment, the story "was as silly as it was false," for if he had truly wanted a token for two young students, "surely less than a talent of silver might serve them."

The witnesses see in the invention the anatomy of a lie that trades on holy things. Gehazi does not merely ask money; he borrows the prophet's authority ("my master hath sent me"), the prophets' fellowship (the sons of the prophets), and the guise of pious generosity, to cover a private theft. Every element is chosen to disarm suspicion, and Naaman, freshly taught to honour Israel's God and his ministers, has no reason to doubt. The convert's very reverence, so lately praised, becomes the ground on which the deceiver plants his fraud.
23:
Naaman not only grants the request but doubles it, and the witnesses linger on the generosity that the servant abuses. "Be content," he urges — "be willing, consent to take," as Charles Ellicott renders it — pressing upon Gehazi two talents where one was asked. John Wesley notes that Gehazi "at first refused it upon a pretence of modesty," the practised feint of one who means all along to take. The convert's open-handedness only draws the deceiver further into his sin, for a modest lie is answered with a lavish kindness, and Gehazi must now carry home twice what he had dared to ask.

The commentators dwell on the sheer weight of the gift, which betrays how much was carried off. Adam Clarke observes that "it required two servants to carry these two talents, for each talent was about a hundred and twenty pounds' weight." Ellicott marks the further courtesy: Naaman "bound two talents of silver in two bags, with two changes of garments, and laid them upon two of his own young men," who "bare them before" Gehazi — the master's own servants pressed into carrying the plunder, and going ahead of the thief as though in his honour. John Gill reads "be content" simply as "be pleased; do not object to it." The doubling is itself a rebuke unspoken: Naaman gives without measure to the servant of a God whose prophet would take nothing, and the very freeness of the gift throws into relief the grasping heart that receives it — the convert lavishing what the deceiver had to lie to obtain.

The witnesses feel the bitter irony the scene stages. Every stroke of Naaman's kindness — the doubled sum, the fine bags, the escort of servants — is a fresh proof of the grace working in the new convert, and every stroke is turned by Gehazi to his own ruin. The Gentile whom Elisha would not let enrich the prophet now heaps silver on the prophet's servant, and the very abundance he gives in gratitude becomes the measure of the leprosy Gehazi is about to inherit. Generosity meets greed, and greed makes even a convert's bounty an instrument of judgment.
24:
Gehazi stows the treasure and dismisses the porters, and the witnesses read the geography of his concealment. Keil and Delitzsch take the place, the Hebrew ophel, as "the well-known hill before the city," and the verb for what he did with the goods as "to bring into safe custody." Charles Ellicott weighs the same word: it is "the mound on which the prophet's house may have stood," or, like the Ophel at Jerusalem, "a fortified hill forming part of the defences surrounding Samaria." He turns even this into evidence, for Elisha's house lay within the city wall (2 Kings 6:30), and "this note of place is also a note of historical truth."

Adam Clarke draws the moral from the shadows. The old versions understand ophel as "a secret, dark, or hiding place," and Clarke finds the fitness of it: "He was doing a deed of darkness, and he sought darkness to conceal it. He no doubt put them in a place little frequented; but the prophet's discerning spirit found him out." Ellicott adds the careful sequence of the verse: Gehazi "let the men go" before he "bestowed" their burdens in the house, dismissing the witnesses before he hid the spoil. John Gill takes the tower for "a fortified place near Samaria, where was a watch," a trusty spot to which he could safely commit the money and clothes. Keil and Delitzsch gloss the operative verb, that Gehazi "brought into safe custody" what he had taken from the porters' hands, laying it up as a man secures a treasure he means to keep — the practised caution of one who reckons concealment to be safety.

The witnesses see in the hiding the folly at the heart of secret sin. Gehazi lays up his gain "in the house" and sends away every eye that saw, thinking himself now safe. But he had reckoned without the seer. As Henry observed, he "applauded his own management of a fraud by which he had imposed, not only upon the prudence of Naaman, but upon Elisha's spirit of discerning" — as Ananias and Sapphira thought to impose upon the apostles. The darkness he sought is no cover from the prophet whose heart had gone with him, and the treasure so carefully secured is already known.
25:
Gehazi returns and takes his place "before his master" as though nothing had happened, and the witnesses catch the brazenness of it. John Gill notes that he went in and stood before Elisha "to know his will, and minister to him, as he had used to do, and as if he had never been from the house." Charles Ellicott marks that Gehazi "was already in the house" and now went in to his master's chamber, coming forward as a servant awaiting orders, the very picture of innocence.

Then comes the prophet's quiet question, "Whence comest thou, Gehazi?" and the flat lie in answer: "Thy servant went no whither." Ellicott renders the Hebrew exactly — "thy servant went not away hither nor thither" — a denial as total as it is false. Keil and Delitzsch set the exchange in its full weight: when Gehazi returned "the lying answer that he had not been anywhere," Elisha "charged him with all that he had done," answering the lie with the searching words, "had not my heart gone, when the man turned from his chariot to meet thee?" They defend this as "the simplest and the only correct interpretation," the reading already given by Theodoret and the Vulgate: the prophet was present in spirit at the very scene Gehazi denies. Ellicott notes how exactly the servant's phrase and the master's will answer each other: Gehazi says "thy servant went not away," and Elisha will reply, in the next verse, that his own heart "went" with him — the same verb turned back upon the liar, so that the denial is refuted in its own words.

The witnesses find in the confrontation the folly of lying to a seer. Gehazi had imposed on Naaman and dismissed the porters and hidden the spoil, and now stands before the one man who cannot be deceived, adding a bald denial to his theft. The lie is not merely to Elisha but, as Henry said of the like sin, an attempt "to lie to the Holy Ghost," for the spirit of prophecy could not be deceived. In the very act of standing innocent before his master, Gehazi is already fully known, and the next words will strip him bare.
26:
Elisha's rebuke lays bare both the deed and the design, and the witnesses marvel at the prophet's discernment. "Went not mine heart with thee, when the man turned again from his chariot to meet thee?" Keil and Delitzsch take it plainly: "was I not in spirit away from here and present there?" — the prophet's heart standing in pointed rebuke against Gehazi's own "I went no whither." Charles Ellicott reads the same severe irony, the prophet adopting his servant's phrase: "nor did my heart go away, when a man turned and alighted from his chariot to meet thee"; "I was there in spirit, and witnessed everything." John Gill gathers the Jewish glosses — the Targum's "by a spirit of prophecy it was shown unto me" — to the same end.

Then the prophet exposes not merely the theft but the whole ambition behind it. "Is it a time to receive money, and to receive garments, and oliveyards, and vineyards, and sheep, and oxen, and menservants, and maidservants?" Adam Clarke notes that Elisha "gave him farther proof of this all-discerning prophetic spirit in telling him what he designed to do with the money" — "to set up a splendid establishment." John Wesley presses the wonder of it: the prophet names these purchases "to inform him that he exactly knew, not only his outward actions, but even his most secret intentions." Ellicott sharpens the question's edge: was that, of all times, "a proper occasion for yielding to your desire of gain, when you were dealing with a heathen? Ought you not to have been studiously disinterested, that he might learn not to confound the prophets of Jehovah with the mercenary diviners of the false gods?"

The witnesses converge on Ellicott's point and Wesley's application. The prophet's disciple is bound, like his master, "to seek, not worldly power, but spiritual," for the age was one of "ardent struggle against the encroachments of paganism," and Gehazi's greed had struck at the very lesson Elisha's refusal was meant to teach. And Wesley turns the searchlight on the reader: "What a folly is it to presume upon sin in hopes of secrecy! When thou goest aside into any by-path, doth not thy own conscience go with thee? Nay, doth not the eye of God go with thee?"
27:
The sentence falls as swiftly as the sin was secret: "The leprosy of Naaman shall cleave unto thee, and unto thy seed for ever." The witnesses feel the fit justice of it. Adam Clarke puts it in a stroke: "Thou hast got Naaman's silver, and thou shalt have Naaman's leprosy," and marks that "the moment the curse was pronounced, the signs of the leprosy began to appear." He then catalogues the sevenfold guilt that earned it — covetousness; pride and vanity, for "he wished to become a great man"; lying to impose on Naaman; in effect selling the cure for money; theft, applying to his own use what was given for his master; dishonouring Elisha by extorting in his name what he had solemnly refused; and crowning all, lying to his master to deny it. Charles Ellicott notes that leprosy, like other skin diseases, is hereditary, so that the curse on the seed is no arbitrary cruelty; and he sets the judgment among the great biblical warnings against greed and falsehood (Acts 5), for "covetousness and lying are never spared in Scripture, and it is well for mankind that it is so."

Over the phrase "for ever" the witnesses take care. Clarke insists the Hebrew leolam "takes in the whole extent of the thing to which it is applied"; "the for ever of Gehazi was till his posterity became extinct," and he dismisses as absurd the notion, which Maundrell went to Judea to test, that leprous descendants survive to this day. John Wesley notes that the leprosy "white as snow" is "the worst kind, and noted by physicians to be incurable" (compare Ex. 4:6; Num. 12:10), and draws the plain lesson: "Those who get money by any way displeasing to God make a dear purchase." John Gill and Keil and Delitzsch agree that the punishment fell not on the avarice only but on the abuse of the prophet's name and the slander of his master.

Henry gathers the whole into the marrow of the chapter. "What was Gehazi profited, though he gained his two talents, when thereby he lost his health, his honour, his peace, his service, and, if repentance prevented not, his soul for ever?" (Job 20). "The getting of treasures by a lying tongue is a vanity tossed to and fro of those that seek death" (Prov. 21:6). And Clarke lifts the eye from Gehazi's ruin to the chapter's great providence — the little maid's captivity, Naaman's healing and conversion — closing with the poet's word: "Behind a frowning providence he hides a smiling face," and the benefit of a religious education, for had not that captive child known the true God, "she had not been the instrument of so great a salvation."
